channel.title: Shubham Bhati | Backend Developer Feed
channel.description: Daily micro-articles and technical tips on Spring Boot, Java and Microservices

item.title: Stop Wasting CPU Cycles on Read-Only Spring Boot Queries
Stop Overpaying for Spring Boot Hosting
item.description:
Most developers think you need a 2GB RAM server to run a Spring Boot app. That is why they spend thousands on cloud providers. You can easily host your Spring Boot API, PostgreSQL and Redis on a $1/month VPS if you tune the JVM.
By default, the JVM is greedy. It grabs memory it doesn't need.
Add these JVM flags to your Dockerfile to cap memory and force aggressive garbage collection:
```bash java -XX:MaxRAMPercentage=50.0 -XX:+UseSerialGC -Xss256k -jar app.jar ```
Using `SerialGC` instead of `G1GC` reduces memory overhead significantly for small applications. Capping the thread stack size (`-Xss256k`) saves precious memory. This lets your container run on a 512MB RAM instance, leaving room for your database and cache.
I used this exact setup to scale a side project to ₹1 Lakh in revenue while paying just ₹80/month for hosting.

item.title: Stop Blaming Hibernate for Slow APIs: Tune Your Connection Pool Instead
Scale Your ₹100/Month VPS with Virtual Threads
item.description:
If you host your app on a cheap ₹100/month VPS, you can't afford heavy OS threads. Standard Spring Boot configuration maps each request to a platform thread. These take up to 1MB of RAM each. With just 100 concurrent requests, your memory spikes and the OS kills your Java process.
Spring Boot 3.2 changes this with Java 21 virtual threads. They run in user space and cost almost nothing in memory.
Switching to them takes exactly one line in `application.properties`:
```properties spring.threads.virtual.enabled=true ```
This single line instructs Tomcat, TaskExecutor and task schedulers to run on virtual threads. Your backend can now handle thousands of concurrent API calls, database queries and network requests on tiny hardware without breaking a sweat.

item.title: Stop Letting Spring Boot Devour Your Cheap VPS RAM
item.description:
Running Spring Boot on a $1 VPS (around ₹80/month) seems impossible because the JVM is notoriously memory-hungry. By default, it will happily eat up all your RAM and trigger the OS out-of-memory killer.
You don't need a heavy instance to run your MVP. You can easily squeeze Spring Boot, PostgreSQL and Redis into a single 512MB RAM machine by tuning your JVM garbage collector and thread limits.
Stop using default settings. Pass these flags when starting your jar:
```bash java -XX:+UseSerialGC -Xss256k -XX:MaxRAMPercentage=50.0 -jar app.jar ```
Using `SerialGC` instead of `G1GC` drastically reduces memory overhead for single-core VPS hosting. Lowering the thread stack size (`-Xss`) to 256k saves massive amounts of RAM because you don't need the default 1MB per thread for typical web apps.
This simple configuration keeps your Spring Boot process idling at under 150MB of RAM, leaving plenty of room for your database. This is how you host dirt cheap, validate your product and scale your revenue.

item.title: Stop Over-allocating HikariCP Connections on Cheap Servers
item.description:
If you're hosting a full-stack Java app on a cheap ₹100/month VPS, default configurations will kill your server. Spring Boot defaults to a HikariCP pool size of 10. If you run your app, Postgres and Redis on the same small machine, you'll run out of RAM fast.
Most APIs don't need 10 active database connections sitting idle. You can easily handle high traffic by shrinking the pool size and reducing the idle timeout. This forces your code to release connections quickly.
Add this to your `application.properties`:
```properties spring.datasource.hikari.maximum-pool-size=4 spring.datasource.hikari.minimum-idle=2 spring.datasource.hikari.idle-timeout=30000 spring.datasource.hikari.connection-timeout=5000 ```
A smaller pool combined with a tight connection timeout keeps your memory footprint low. We've used this exact setup to handle thousands of requests daily on a single-core VPS without spending a fortune. It keeps your app lightweight, fast and cheap to run.

item.title: Optimizing Spring Boot App Performance
Stop Using Spring Boot Defaults on Cheap ₹100 VMs
item.description:
When you run Spring Boot on a cheap 1GB RAM virtual machine, default configurations will crash your app. By default, HikariCP sets the maximum connection pool size to 10. If you don't change this, multiple microservices running on the same cheap machine will quickly exhaust PostgreSQL connections and memory.
For a tiny VPS, a pool size of 2 or 3 is plenty to handle hundreds of concurrent requests.
Update your `application.properties`:
```properties spring.datasource.hikari.maximum-pool-size=3 spring.datasource.hikari.minimum-idle=1 spring.datasource.hikari.idle-timeout=10000 spring.datasource.hikari.leak-detection-threshold=2000 ```
This tweak prevents PostgreSQL from choking on idle connections, keeps your total memory footprint under 300MB and saves you from paying for expensive server upgrades.

item.title: Spring Boot Security: Don't Expose That Sensitive Property
item.description:
Ever found yourself accidentally committing sensitive keys or passwords to your Git repo? It's a common slip-up. In Spring Boot, the easiest way to avoid this for things like API keys or database credentials is to keep them out of your main `application.properties` or `application.yml`.
Instead, use environment variables. Spring Boot automatically picks them up. For example, if you have a property `app.external.api.key`, you can set an environment variable named `APP_EXTERNAL_API_KEY`.
Your `application.properties` would still declare it:
```properties app.external.api.key=${EXTERNAL_API_KEY:default_fallback_key} ```
This way, your code is clean, your repo is safe and you can manage your secrets per environment (dev staging prod) without touching the codebase.

item.title: Stop Holding DB Connections Hostage
